chapter_context.summary: A beautiful Hebrew poem of prayer and worship (Psalm 98) belonging to Book IV (Moses & Anonymous). This psalm reflects on God's eternity contrasted with human frailty, His absolute kingship, and praise for His wonderful works. It leads the worshiper through deep emotional expressions, offering comfort, confidence, and ultimate praise to the Almighty.

outline.text: Opening Call to Praise, Lament, or Heartfelt Petition
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Opening Call to Praise, Lament, or Heartfelt Petition (98:1-3), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Psalms.

outline.text: Development of Core Themes, Theological Reflection, and Trust
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Development of Core Themes, Theological Reflection, and Trust (98:4-6), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Psalms.

outline.text: Concluding Doxology, Triumph, or Statement of Complete Assurance
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Concluding Doxology, Triumph, or Statement of Complete Assurance (98:7-9), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Psalms.

key_themes.title: God's Sovereignty and Eternity
key_themes.description: This Psalm explores key facets of god's sovereignty and eternity, revealing how worshipers connect with God in all of life's seasons.

key_themes.title: The Way of the Righteous
key_themes.description: A recurring theme contrasting the path of the faithful, who trust in God's counsel, with the destructive path of the wicked.

devotional.question: Reflecting on the expressions of Psalm 98, how can you bring your honest feelings and prayers before the Lord today?
How does this psalm encourage you to trust in God's faithfulness and ultimate deliverance in your current circumstances?

cultural_historical.title: Hebrew Poetic Parallelism
cultural_historical.text: Unlike western poetry based on rhyme or meter, Hebrew poetry relies on parallelism—where the second line repeats, expands, or contrasts the thought of the first.
